segments.s567-g137: به ایران ... نماند is a locative fronting: به ایران places the scene (here rendered “in Iran”) and نماند (perfective/past negative of ماندن) says that none 'remained'. The enumeration زن و مرد و کودک lists survivors in the conventional order “woman and man and child” with و as simple conjunctions; the verb follows the list and applies to all three.
segments.s567-g138: همان چیز بسیار و اندک نماند is a summary clause: همان چیز (literally “that/same thing”) here functions as a generic ‘anything’, followed by the polar pair بسیار و اندک “great and small / many and few”; نماند repeats the negative ‘remained not’. The order places the general noun (چیز) before the qualifiers (بسیار و اندک) and then the verb.
segments.s567-g139: نبود آگهی در میان سپاه is a negative-existential statement: نبود (past of بودن with negative sense) means “there was not”; آگهی is the thing lacking (“news/information”); در میان سپاه is a locative phrase “among the host/army” that specifies where the lack of news was felt.
segments.s567-g140: نه مرده نه زنده introduces an appositive clarification: ‘neither dead nor alive’ (an expression of uncertainty about persons’ fates). The short prepositional phrase ز شاپور شاه attaches to this clause: ز here means “concerning/from/by” (poetic use) and links the uncertainty to شاپور شاه (King Shapur). The verb is omitted but understood from the previous clause (elliptical construction).
segments.s567-g141: گریزان همه شهر ایران ز روم is a verbless (participial) clause where گریز-ان (“fleeing” or “in flight”) describes the state of همه شهر ایران (“all the cities of Iran”); ز روم (‘from the Romans/Byzantium’) gives the source or cause. Persian permits such participial/nominal phrases to stand as predicates.
segments.s567-g142: ز مردم تهی شد همه مرز و بوم has the resultive verb phrase تهی شد (“became empty/was emptied of”) with ز مردم marking what was lacking (literally “empty of people”). The postposed subject همه مرز و بوم (“all border and homeland”) follows in typical Persian word order where the verb can precede or follow the subject for emphasis.
segments.s567-g143: از ایران بی‌اندازه ترسا شدند is structured with از ایران marking origin/scene (“from Iran”), بی‌اندازه as an adverbial intensifier “excessively,” and ترسا (a substantive/adjective ‘pious person’ in older usage) as the predicate nominal; شدند is the past plural of شدن, here ‘became’ — together: “they became excessively pious” or “turned into extreme ascetics.”
segments.s567-g144: همه مرز پیش سکوبا شدند literally places the subject همه مرز (“all the border/frontier”) before the prepositional phrase پیش سکوبا (“in the presence of / before Sekuba”) with شدند (“became” / “came to be”) as the verb; پیش functions here as “to the presence of” or “before” implying motion or appearance before the named entity سکوبا. Note سکوبا is an obscure/rare term/name in the text and is treated here as a proper noun or special noun.
segments.s567-g25: بباشد is an optative/subjunctive form meaning 'may it be' (a wish), followed by همه بودنی 'all that exists' (همه modifying بودنی). بی‌گمان is an adverb 'without doubt' that qualifies the whole clause. Word order is classical: optative verb + noun phrase + adverb.
segments.s567-g26: نتابیم is first-person plural present/subjunctive of تابیدن with negative prefix ن- meaning 'we cannot/bear not to' or 'we would not endure'. با + گردش آسمان = 'with the turning/revolution of the sky' (گردش = rotation/revolution, آسمان = heaven/sky). The clause states inability to endure the heavens' motion.
segments.s567-g27: چنین 'thus' introduces the manner. داد here is the past verb 'gave' and پاسـخ 'answer/reply' is its object; گرانمایه qualifies شاه ('the noble/esteemed king'). Classical Persian often places the object before the verb, so the phrase reads literally 'thus gave answer the noble king.'
segments.s567-g28: که introduces the content clause of the king's answer ('that'). دادار is used as 'the Creator' (a name for God). باشد is a subjunctive/optative 'may (he) be' or 'may (he) grant', and ز here is the poetic preposition 'from/against'. هر بد نگاه = 'every evil gaze/evil eye.' Together the clause expresses a wish: 'may the Creator (protect us) from every evil glance.'
segments.s567-g29: که is a relative/connector 'who/that'. گردان is an attributive participle/adjective 'revolving' modifying بلند آسمان 'lofty/heavenly sky'. آفرید is the past verb 'created.' Word order places the descriptive phrase before the verb, typical of classical Persian.
segments.s567-g30: توانایی و ناتوان are two coordinated nouns 'the able/capable and the unable/powerless.' آفرید repeats (or applies) as the past verb 'created' for both items; the verb comes after the coordinated nouns, giving a simple paratactic list 'created A and B.'
segments.s567-g31: بگسترد is an older/perfective past form 'he spread/extended' (the prefix بـ is an archaic verbal prefix). بر پادشاهیش = 'over his kingship/realm' (پادشاهیش = his kingship), and داد here is a noun 'justice' (not the verb 'gave'). The whole means 'he spread justice over his realm.'
segments.s567-g32: همی بود is a classical way to mark imperfective/past existence ('there was/for a while he was'); یک چند = 'a short while / a few (moments)' (an idiom meaning 'for some time'); بی‌رنج و شاد are predicative adjectives 'untroubled and happy.' The clause describes a temporary peaceful, joyful state.
segments.s567-g89: به + Noun (به قیصر) is the indirect object 'to the Caesar' placed before the verb, which is normal in Persian. چنین گفت is a fixed order 'said thus/so' where گفت is simple past 'he said'. کای is an archaic vocative 'O', addressing the addressee, followed by سرفراز 'exalted/proud one'.
segments.s567-g90: یکی نو سخن is an indefinite noun phrase 'a certain/new speech'. بشنو is an imperative 'listen/hear' directed at the addressee. از من 'from me' gives the source, and به راز is an adverbial phrase 'in secret' (به here marks the circumstance 'in/with').
segments.s567-g91: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause 'that/ who'. این نامور مرد بازارگان is a noun phrase 'this famed man, a merchant' with the demonstrative این before the nouns; نامور is an epithet 'renowned/famed'.
segments.s567-g92: که again functions as 'who' introducing a subordinate clause describing the man. دیبا (object) precedes the verb فروشد, which here is a 3rd-person present/past literary form 'sells' (an archaic/poetic form). به دینارگان is a dative/benefactive phrase 'to the dinar-owners' = 'to the wealthy'.
segments.s567-g93: شهنشاه شاپور گویم literally 'I call (him) Shahanshah Shapur' — گویم is 1st-person present 'I say/call'. که here introduces the clause that follows ('who/that is...'). هست is the copula 'is'. Note the title precedes the personal name, as is common in older Persian.
segments.s567-g94: به governs a series of nouns linked by و and marks the domain or respect in which he is distinguished: گفتار (speech), دیدار (appearance/meeting), فر (royal glory), نشست (courtly sitting/assembly). The single به applies to each noun in the list.
segments.s567-g95: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. بشنید is the past tense 'he heard'. The clause's predicate تیره شد is 'became dark/bleak' — here describing a change of mood or countenance upon hearing the speech; سخن is the object 'the words/the speech'.
segments.s567-g96: همی is a poetic adverbial particle adding immediacy/emphasis. چشمش 'his eye(s)' is the subject, از روی او is a phrase 'toward/at him' (از ... روی = regarding/on account of his face/his person), and خیره شد is a compound/predicative phrase 'became fixed/stared' indicating astonishment or a fixed stare.
segments.s567-g81: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a subordinate clause. The clause has a relatively direct classical order: subject شاپور, adverbial نزدیک قیصر (near the Caesar), and the past-tense verb رسید 'reached/arrived' at the end.
segments.s567-g82: بکرد + آفرینی is a verbal phrase meaning 'he uttered/said “Afārīn”'; بکرد is the past auxiliary/verb 'he did/said' and آفرینی is the exclamation/praise. چنان چون سزید is a comparative/adverbial phrase 'in such a way as was deserved/befitting' — چنان/چون = 'such/as' and سزید (from سزا) expresses 'befitted/was deserved'.
segments.s567-g83: نگه کرد is a verbal compound 'cast a look / looked' with کرد as the light verb. The subject قیصر follows the verb (a common classical ordering here), and به شاپور گرد is the directional phrase 'toward Shapur' where گرد functions adverbially 'around/toward'.
segments.s567-g84: ز خوبی introduces a causal/agentive phrase 'because of / from (his) beauty' using the poetic preposition ز. دل و دیده (heart and eye(s)) form the thing given; او را (him) with را marks the recipient/object, and سپرد is the past 'entrusted/gave over'. In context this means the Caeser gave his heart and eyes to (i.e. devoted them to) Shapur. The compact word order omits a possessive suffix that modern prose would make explicit.
segments.s567-g85: بفرمود تا combines the honorific past 'he commanded/said' with تا introducing a subordinate clause expressing purpose or command ('that/in order that'). خوان و می‌ساختند uses خوان 'the banquet/table' and the imperfect/continuous-form می‌ساختند 'they were making/setting up' (in narrative past sense here), describing what was to be prepared.
segments.s567-g86: ز is the poetic preposition 'from / concerning', starting a new short clause. بیگانه 'the stranger/foreigner' modifies ایوان 'the iwan/porch', and بپرداختند is a past verb meaning 'they attended to / took care of / furnished' (here: they saw to the stranger's iwan). The clause succinctly states who was dealt with and what was done.
segments.s567-g87: جفادیده is a compound adjective 'wronged/afflicted' modifying ایرانیی 'the Iranian (man/people)'; بد به روم is a prepositional phrase 'ill toward Rome' meaning 'ill-treated/by Rome' (classical/poetic phrasing places بد before the prepositional phrase). The sequence compactly states that the Iranian suffered at the hands of Rome.
segments.s567-g88: چنانچون is a comparative conjunction 'just as / insofar as', here linking to a descriptive clause introduced by the copula بود 'was' (past). مرد بیداد و شوم is a nominal phrase 'a man of injustice and ill‑omen' (مرد = man; بیداد = injustice/cruelty; شوم = ill‑omened/peevish), giving the characterisation; the copula ties the comparison to that characterization.
segments.s567-g73: دگر followed by the object marker را marks 'the other/the rest' as a definite object. فروشم is 1st‑person present/subjunctive form of فروختن used here to express the speaker's intention/plan ('I sell / I will sell'). به زر و به سیم is a simple prepositional phrase 'for/to gold and to silver' (به = 'for/to', زر = 'gold', سیم = 'silver') joined by و 'and'.
segments.s567-g74: به قیصر introduces the destination/recipient 'to Caesar'. پناهم is پناه ('refuge') with the enclitic -م; here it functions as the thing one would seek (object of the verb). نپیچم is the negative of پیچیدن with prefix ن- plus 1st‑person ending -م and here means 'I do not (turn/ resort)'; classical Persian uses پیچیدن in the sense 'to turn to/seek (refuge)'. ز بیم = 'because of/out of fear' (ز = 'because of', بیم = 'fear').
segments.s567-g75: بخرم is 1st‑person present/subjunctive of خریدن ('I buy' / 'I will buy') used to express the speaker's intent. هرانچم is an older compound meaning 'whatever (amount/thing) I have/need' (literally 'each/whatever that which'). بباید (بایـد with the prosthetic ب-) expresses necessity 'must/ought', so the clause reads 'I buy whatever I must from Rum' (ز روم = 'from Rum').
segments.s567-g76: This is a short sequence of place‑direction phrases: روم (Rum/Byzantium) + سوی ایران 'toward Iran'. ز آباد بوم is a genitival/ablative phrase 'from the prosperous homeland' (ز = 'from/of', آباد = 'prosperous/inhabited', بوم = 'homeland/native soil'). Word order is typical poetic inversion — several place markers are stacked.
segments.s567-g77: ز درگاه = 'from the threshold/doorway' (ز = 'from', درگاه = 'threshold/door or court'); برخاست is a past simple 'rose/stood up'. مرد کهن is the noun phrase 'an old/ancient man' (کهن = 'old/ancient' in poetic register). The verb برخاست governs the following noun phrase as its subject.
segments.s567-g78: بر قیصر آمد is a compact sequence 'he came toward/approached the Caesar' (بر here = 'toward/to' + آمد 'came'). بگفت is the past-tense narrative form 'he said' (ب‑ prefix is the old past narrative marker), and این سخن = 'these words' or 'this speech' (این = 'this', سخن = 'speech/words').
segments.s567-g79: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past description 'he commanded/said (respectfully)' (the b‑ prefix marks narrative politeness). تا here introduces the result 'so/that', and پرده برداشتند is a past plural 'they removed/lifted the curtain' (برداشتند = they took away/removed).
segments.s567-g80: ز در = 'from the door/at the doorway' (ز = 'from/of', در = 'door/entrance'); سوی قیصرش = 'toward his/their Caesar' (سوی = 'toward', قیصرش = 'his Caesar' or 'the Caesar belonging to him/the court'); بگذاشتند is past plural 'they placed/put' — the clause says they placed (him or something) by the door in front of the Caesar. The exact direct object is implicit in the narrative.
segments.s567-g65: به + بازارگانی is a prepositional phrase 'to/into the merchants' market' (here بازارگانی = merchantry/market). برفتم is a first-person past verb 'I went' (classical prefix بر- + رفتن) placed after the place phrase. ز جز is a short idiom 'apart from / except' (ز = from, جز = except).
segments.s567-g66: یکی کاروان دارم = 'I have one caravan' with numeric/indefinite یکی before the noun and دارم (present) as the verb 'I have.' از خز و بز enumerates the caravan's contents with از marking composition/contents ('of' / 'consisting of') and و joining خز (furs) and بز (goats).
segments.s567-g67: کنون آمدستم = 'now I have come' (کنون 'now'; آمدستم is first-person past/ perfect-like 'I came/approached'). بدین بارگاه is 'to this court' (ب = to, دین = orthographic/demonstrative 'this', بارگاه = palace/court). The preposition + demonstrative combine to mark the destination.
segments.s567-g68: مگر here functions as a concessive/expectational particle ('except / unless / perhaps' → reading: 'in the hope that' or 'unless there is'), introducing the desired outcome. نزد قیصر = 'in the presence of / at the side of the Caesar (emperor)'. گشاینده راه is a noun phrase 'the opener of the way' (agent noun گشاینده + راه 'path'), i.e. an intermediary or one who will clear a road.
segments.s567-g69: ازین بار = 'from this load' (از + این (written ین) + بار 'load/cargo'). چیزی = 'anything / something' as the item quantified; کش here is an archaic or enclitic element that does not match the provided senses (functionally contributes to the negative/limiting sense in context), and اندر = 'within / among'. خورست is a stative 'is edible / fit to be eaten' (meaning: there is nothing edible in the load).
segments.s567-g70: همه = 'all' (subject); گوهر و آلت is a coordinated noun phrase 'gems and implements/equipment'; لشکرست is a predicative expression (لشکر + است contracted) meaning 'is (for) an army' or 'belonging to an army' — i.e. the cargo consists of jewels and military gear.
segments.s567-g71: پذیرد and سپارد are third-person singular verbs here ('he accepts' / 'he entrusts' or optative/formulaic requests), listed in sequence showing two actions: accepting and depositing. به گنجور گنج sets the direction/recipient: 'the treasure into the treasury' (به = to, گنجور = treasury/treasure-house, گنج = treasure).
segments.s567-g72: بدان = به + آن 'at that / by that'; شاد باشم = 'I shall be glad' (شاد 'happy', باشم = present-subjunctive/optative 'may I be / I will be'); ندارم به رنج = 'I have no need of toil' (ندارم 'I do not have', به رنج 'to/for hardship' — i.e. I will not suffer toil).
segments.s567-g33: چو is a subordinating conjunction meaning “when.” آباد شد is a past verbal phrase “became prosperous.” ز (here written as زو in the line) is the poetic preposition meaning “from/through/because of (him).” همه مرز و بوم is a noun phrase “all the borders and the land/country”; Persian places the whole phrase (object/affected domain) after the prepositional phrase. The sequence thus reads: “When, through him, all the borders and the land became prosperous.”
segments.s567-g34: چنان آرزو کرد is a main clause: چنان “thus/so” + آرزو کرد “he wished/made a wish.” The following کاید به روم is a subordinate desiderative clause expressing the wished-for action: here it functions like “that he might go to Rome.” کاید is an archaic/poetic form expressing the wish/optative (not a common modern verb form), and به روم is the destination “to Rome/Byzantium.”
segments.s567-g35: ببیند here is a present/subjunctive form of دیدن used in a subordinate clause governed by the preceding desire: it means “to see / to find out” (i.e. “he wished to see”). که introduces the content clause “that / whether.” قیصر سزاوار هست is a predicative clause: قیصر (Caesar) + سزاوار (worthy) + هست (copula ‘is’). The whole means “he wished to see whether Caesar was worthy.”
segments.s567-g36: This is a nominal list introduced (in the line) as the things of which Caesar might be worthy. ابا is an archaic/poetic item here; in context it functions to introduce the list (roughly ‘of / to have’), but the exact sense is not the common modern one and is therefore marked uncertain. لشکر و گنج و نیروی دست is a coordinated list: لشکر “army,” گنج “treasure,” نیروی دست literally “strength/force of the hand” i.e. military power/manpower — repeated و conjunctions simply join the items.
segments.s567-g37: همان راز (the same secret) is the direct object phrase. بگشاد is a past/perfective verbal form from گشودن (to open/reveal) with the old prefix بـ; here it means “he revealed” or “he opened up.” با کدخدای is a prepositional phrase “to/with the village headman” indicating the person to whom the secret was revealed. Word order places the object before the verb and then the indirect object phrase.
segments.s567-g38: This clause gives the instruction or accompaniment: یک پهلوان ‘one/a champion.’ گرد here is a short, polyvalent archaic form from roots meaning ‘to go about / gather / assemble’; in this context it functions as an action word instructing to muster/bring together a hero. با داد و رای is an adverbial phrase “with justice and counsel” (داد = justice, رای = counsel/plan) describing how the champion should be chosen or the mission conducted.
segments.s567-g39: همه راز و اندیشه is a coordinated object “all secrets and thoughts.” با او is the indirect object “to/with him” (the village headman). بگفت is the past tense verb ‘he told/relayed’ (بـ- prefix + گفت). The sentence therefore says he related all his secrets and thoughts to that man.
segments.s567-g40: همی داشت is a habitual/past-continuous construction: همی (ongoing/habitual marker) + داشت (imperfective ‘had/kept’) — here expressing that he kept on doing something. از هرکس اندر نهفت is a prepositional phrase + verb/participle: از هرکس ‘from everyone’ + اندر (poetic ‘in/among’) + نهفت (hidden/concealed) — together meaning he kept (the secrets) concealed from everyone else. The whole clause: “he continually kept [them] hidden from any other person.”
segments.s567-g129: کلید is the direct object 'the key'. در خانه او functions as a possessive/locative phrase 'of his house' (literally 'in his house') modifying کلید. را is the definite object marker attaching to کلید (so the core structure is ‘کلید ... را سپرد’). سپرد is the simple past (3rd sg.) of سپردن meaning 'entrusted/handed over'.
segments.s567-g130: به introduces the manner/means or direction here ('into/in'), and چرم اندرون بسته is a participial/compound description: چرم 'leather', اندرون 'inside/inner part', بسته 'bound/closed' (past participle), i.e. 'wrapped/tied into a leather inner pouch'. شاپور is the agent/name. گرد is a poetic/archaic form used in Shahnameh (here not the common senses 'around' or 'dust'); it functions like an old past-form (cf. کرد) indicating the action was done by Shapur: 'Shapur did (this)'.
segments.s567-g131: همان روز is a temporal adverbial 'that same day'. ازان مرز = 'from that border/frontier' (ازان = 'from that'). لشکر is the object 'the army', and براند is the (poetic) past form of راندن 'to drive away' (3rd sg.), so together 'he drove the army away from that frontier.'
segments.s567-g132: ورا is the unstressed object pronoun 'him/it' (to him/it). بسته is a past participle meaning 'bound/tied' describing the state ('having been bound'). در پوست = 'in a hide/skin' (در = 'in', پوست = 'hide/skin'), and آنجا 'there'. بماند is the past narrative sense of ماندن ('was left/remained'), so the clause means 'he bound him in a skin and left him there.'
segments.s567-g133: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. قیصر is the subject 'Caesar'. به نزدیک ایران combines the preposition به + adjective نزدیک + proper noun ایران to mean 'near/approaching Iran'. رسید is the past 3rd sg. of رسیدن 'arrived/reached'. The clause = 'When Caesar reached near Iran.'
segments.s567-g134: سپه is 'the army/host' as subject. یک به یک is an adverbial phrase 'one by one'. تیغ کین is a genitival phrase 'swords of vengeance' (کین qualifying تیغ). برکشید is past (3rd sg.) of برکشیدن 'to raise/draw' (here 'drew [their] swords'). The whole clause: 'the host drew vengeful swords one by one.'
segments.s567-g135: از ایران is the source phrase 'from Iran'. همی is the narrative/progressive particle indicating repeated or continuous action (common in epic Persian). برد here functions as a past verb 'took/carried off' (بردن) meaning 'took away' (note: the provided sense list did not include this verbal sense). رومی is the agent 'the Roman/byzantine', and اسیر is 'a captive' (object). Together: 'the Roman(s) kept taking captives from Iran.'
segments.s567-g136: نبود is the negative past existential 'there was not' / 'there was no'. آن یلان را fronted means 'those heroes' with را marking them as definite/object/topic. کسی here is used in the sense 'anyone/no one' (in negative contexts meaning 'no one'). دستگیر is used as a verbal/adjectival form 'to seize/arrest' (i.e. 'no one to seize them'). The clause: 'there was no one to capture those heroes.'
segments.s567-g121: همان is a demonstrative meaning 'that' or 'that same' modifying تخت قیصر ('Caesar's throne'). نیایدش is the classical negative verb نیاید ('does not come/appear') plus the enclitic -ش ('to him'), so نیایدش یاد literally 'the remembrance does not come to him' = 'he does not remember'. یاد is the noun 'memory/remembrance' and here is the object of نیایدش; poetic word order places یاد after the verb.
segments.s567-g122: کسی را uses را as an object/definiteness marker but here is emphatic ('anyone/no one'); کجا نیست is a rhetorical inversion meaning 'is not anywhere' or 'there is nowhere'. قیصر نژاد is a genitival/idom phrase 'of Caesar's stock/lineage'. Together the clause states there is nowhere anyone of Caesar's lineage.
segments.s567-g123: زن قیصر is a genitive construction: 'the wife of Caesar'. آن خانه را is the definite object 'that house' (را marks the object). در here is the noun 'door' (classical Persian uses در for 'door'), and ببست is the past tense 'shut/closed' (3rd singular), so 'the wife of Caesar shut the door of that house.'
segments.s567-g124: به ایوان دگر جای is a locative phrase 'to/into another place on the iwan' (به + noun). بودش is the enclitic form of بودن ('it was' or 'there was to her') used here to set the scene, and نشست is the past verb 'sat' or 'took a seat'. The sequence means she moved to and sat in another spot on the porch.
segments.s567-g125: یکی is an indefinite 'one' meaning 'a certain', and ماه‌رخ is an adjective-noun 'moon-faced' (beautiful). بود is the past copula 'was'. گنجور is an epithet meaning 'treasure-laden' or 'rich in treasures' describing her, and اوی is an archaic enclitic pronoun referring back to the woman ('she'). The clause says 'there was a certain moon-faced, treasure-laden woman.'
segments.s567-g126: گزیده is an adjectival or appositive 'chosen' / 'select' (i.e. of special status). به هر کار is a prepositional phrase 'in/for every affair', and دستور اوی literally 'her command/order' (دستور = 'order, command'; اوی is the possessive/enclitic 'her'). The clause states that she was a chosen one whose command applied in every matter.
segments.s567-g127: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause 'who/for', ز is the classical preposition 'from/of', ایرانیان is 'the Iranians'. داشتی is an archaic past form 'had/possessed' (here 'had descent'), او is the pronoun 'she', and نژاد is 'lineage/stock'. Together: 'who had lineage from the Iranians.'
segments.s567-g128: پدر بر پدر بر is a repetitive idiom 'father upon father' meaning successive ancestors. همی is an iterative/adverbial particle meaning 'continually/indeed', داشت is the past verb 'had' (possession/holding), and یاد here means 'remembrance' or 'record' (i.e. knowledge of ancestry). The phrase says she possessed the remembrance/record of father after father — she could trace her ancestry through successive fathers.
segments.s567-g113: بدان is an archaic/prepositional contraction meaning 'to/into that' (به + آن) and introduces the location. جای تنگ is the noun phrase 'narrow place' (جای = place, تنگ = narrow). اندر انداختند is a past-tense verbal phrase: اندر (in/within) + انداختند (they threw/put) — literally 'they cast (him) into that narrow place.' Word order is typical classical Persian: location phrase before the verb.
segments.s567-g114: در خانه را is the definite object (در = 'door' here, خانه = 'house', را = object marker) meaning 'the house's door.' قفل بر ساختند is literally 'they made/placed a lock upon' (قفل = lock, بر = on/upon, ساختند = they made/put), so the whole clause means 'they put a lock on the house door.' Note ساختند is past tense, third-person plural.
segments.s567-g115: کلیدش is 'his key' (کلید + possessive -ش). به کدبانوی خانه داد is 'gave to the housekeeper of the house' (به = to, کدبانو = housewife/housemistress, خانه = house; داد = gave). The verb is past tense (they/he gave); the subject is omitted and understood from context.
segments.s567-g116: تنش را is the definite direct object 'his body' (تن = body, ش = his, را = object marker). بدان is the archaic prepositional form 'to that' (ب + دان) introducing the indirect object. چرم بیگانه is 'a foreign/strange hide or leather' (چرم = leather, بیگانه = stranger/foreign). داد is the past verb 'gave/delivered.' The clause literally reads 'they gave his body to that foreign hide' (an odd collocation in modern English, but grammatically: direct object + indirect prepositional phrase + past verb).
segments.s567-g117: به زن گفت = 'he told the woman' (به = to, زن = woman, گفت = said/told). چندان modifies the following imperative as 'so much/that much.' دهش here functions as an older/formal imperative 'give him' (ده = give, with enclitic object); نان و آب = 'bread and water' — object of the imperative. The clause is an indirect speech order: he told the woman to give that amount.
segments.s567-g118: که introduces a purpose/result clause 'so that.' از داشتن زو is a compressed classical phrasing meaning 'from possessing him' (از = from; داشتن = having/possessing; ز + و here represent poetic contraction of از او = 'from him'). نگیرد شتاب is a negative subjunctive/optative construction: نگیرد (n + گیرد) = 'let her not take' / 'she should not take' and شتاب = 'haste' — together meaning 'so that she will not hasten' or 'so that she will not be quick to (do something, e.g., deprive or remove him).' The clause expresses purpose: give enough so she won't rush to act.
segments.s567-g119: اگر introduces a conditional 'if.' زنده ماند is a two-word predicate 'remained alive' (زنده = alive, ماند = remained/lasted) in past-tense form used for a future/hypothetical condition. به یک چندگاه means 'for a short while' (به = for, یک چندگاه = a short time/for a while). Together: 'If he remains alive for a little while...'.
segments.s567-g120: بداند is a present/future-oriented form of 'to know/realize' (here rendered 'he will know' or 'he will come to know'). مگر in this classical position is a small particle introducing the focused content that follows (roughly 'namely' / 'indeed'). ارج تخت و کلاه is a noun phrase: ارج = honor/value, تخت = throne, کلاه = crown (metonymically 'royal dignity'). So the clause means 'he will learn the honor/value of throne and crown.'
segments.s567-g17: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so' modifying the verb. داد is the past tense of دادن 'gave' (here used as 'said/gave'), followed by the object پاسخ بدوی 'an original/first reply'. شاپور is the subject (name). Word order is slightly inverted for poetic effect: adverb + verb + subject + object.
segments.s567-g18: که introduces direct speech ('that' / 'and said'), and ای is the vocative particle addressing someone. مرد داننده و راه‌جوی is the vocative phrase 'O knowledgeable man and seeker (of the road)'; و simply links the two epithets.
segments.s567-g19: چه is an interrogative/exclamatory 'what'. چارست is an archaic/polymorphic item here; the exact classical sense is uncertain in the provided senses (so marked below). تا introduces the subordinate clause 'until...', followed by این 'this', ز من 'from me' and بگذرد, a present/subjunctive form of گذشتن 'to pass' used here as 'passes / may pass'.
segments.s567-g20: تنم is 'my body' (fronted for emphasis; the lemma in the list is non‑matching so sense is marked -1). اختر بد 'ill/evil star' is the agent phrase; به پی 'to/in pursuit of, to follow' and نسپرد is the past tense negative of سپردن 'to entrust/hand over' (here 'did not entrust' / 'did not let'). The verb is third‑person past and governs the preceding noun phrases.
segments.s567-g21: ستاره‌شمر is the agent 'the astrologer'. گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' (the provided sense list gave a related noun sense; here it is used as the verb). کای شهریار is a vocative: کای = 'O' and شهریار = 'sovereign/king'.
segments.s567-g22: ازین = از + این 'from this' (ین shown as orthographic variant). گردش چرخ is a genitive phrase 'the turning/circuit of the wheel (the heavens)'; چرخ here denotes the celestial wheel/sky. ناپایدار is an adjective 'unstable/transient' modifying گردش چرخ.
segments.s567-g23: به + [مورد] marks the instrument/means 'by/with': به مردی و دانش = 'by manliness (mardi, here 'manhood') and knowledge'. نیابی is a negative subjunctive/optative form meaning 'you will not find/obtain' (grammatically listed as subjunctive/imperative in the senses). گذر is the noun 'passage' or 'pass'.
segments.s567-g24: خردمند‌گر is a fused conditional: خردمند + گر (if the wise ...) i.e. 'if the wise man ...'. مرد پرخاشخر is the predicate 'a quarrelsome man' (مرد as noun with adjective پرخاشخر). Together they form the protasis: 'if the wise man is quarrelsome.'
segments.s567-g97: نگهبانش برکرد و با کس نگفت is a clause with a subject + verb + negative speech phrase. نگهبانش (نگهبان + -ش) is the possessed/definite subject 'his guard'; برکرد is an older/poetic past tense meaning 'rose/stood up' (literally 'raised himself'). و با کس نگفت places the conjunction + preposition before کس and a past negative verb: با کس 'with anyone' and نگفت 'did not say/said nothing' (here meaning 'did not speak/tell anyone'). The order is Subject — Verb — (and) Prepositional phrase — Verb (negative), typical of epic Persian word order.
segments.s567-g98: همی داشت آن راز را در نهفت is a continuative/perfective construction describing an ongoing state. همی داشت is the progressive/periphrastic past ('was keeping' or 'kept continually'), آن راز را is the definite object 'that secret' (را marks the object), and در نهفت is a verbless adjectival phrase 'hidden inside' (در + نهفت). Together: 'he was keeping that secret hidden.'
segments.s567-g99: چو شد مست برخاست شاپور شاه is a temporal clause introduced by چو 'when'. شد + مست is 'became drunk' (شد as 'became' + predicate مست), and برخاست is the past action 'rose/stood up'. The final apposition شاپور شاه names the subject (Shapur, the king); the order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect but reads 'When Shapur the king became drunk, he rose.'
segments.s567-g100: همی داشت قیصر مر او را نگاه uses همی داشت as the progressive/periphrastic 'kept/was keeping'. قیصر is the agent 'Caesar' (subject). نگاه is the noun 'gaze' used as the thing kept; او را is the definite object 'at him'. The short form مر here is an archaic/clitic element (see senses) occurring between subject and object; the whole means 'Caesar kept his gaze on him.'
segments.s567-g101: بیامد نگهبان و او را گرفت is two coordinated actions in the simple past. بیامد 'came' (verb) names the arrival of the guard; نگهبان is the subject. و او را گرفت 'and seized him' uses او را as the definite object and گرفت as the past verb 'seized/laid hold of'.
segments.s567-g102: که شاپور نرسی توی‌ای شگفت is reported speech or an explanatory clause introduced by که. شاپور is the vocative/name addressed. نرسی is a verbal form (here expressive of 'reach/attain' or 'do not go so as to reach') and in poetry can carry an optative/imperative shading; توی‌ای ('in/among') + شگفت ('marvel/wonder') completes the phrase 'among such a wonder.' Overall the clause functions as the guard's admonition—'O Shapur, do not go among such marvels.'
segments.s567-g103: به جای زنان برد و دستش ببست contains two coordinated past actions. به جای زنان برد literally '(he) took (him) to the place of the women' (برد here is the past of بردن 'to take'—poetic context gives the meaning 'led/took into the women's quarters'). و دستش ببست 'and bound his hands' uses دست + possessive -ش 'his hand(s)' and ببست (poetic past) 'bound/tied'. The object (him) is implied from context.
segments.s567-g104: به مردی ز دام بلا کس نجست is a negative/indefinite remark about rescue. به مردی 'to/for a man' (or 'no man') + ز دام بلا 'from the snare/trap of calamity' describes the danger; کس نجست is an old/poetic way of saying 'no one sought/attempted' (کس = no one here, نجست = did not attempt/seek). Together: 'no man tried to rescue him from the snare of affliction.'
segments.s567-g9: نگه کرد is a past transitive compound (نگه + کرد) meaning 'he cast/gave a look'; روشن immediately follows as an adverbial/adjectival qualifier 'bright/clear' modifying that look; به قلب اسد is a prepositional phrase ('into/to the heart of Asad') showing the target of the gaze, with اسد a proper name in the genitive position after قلب.
segments.s567-g10: که introduces an explanatory / relative clause ('for/since/that'); هست is the copula 'is' stating identity; او is the pronoun 'he'; نماینده is the predicate noun 'representative/embodiment'; فتح و جد is a coordinated noun phrase ('victory/conquest and seriousness/earnestness'), joined by و. Word order is standard copular: 'that he is the representative of X and Y.'
segments.s567-g11: بدان is an imperative 'know this' (ب + دان = be-know); تا introduces a contingent clause here ('if/should/so that'); رسد is a 3rd-person present/future/optative verb 'reach/attend/befall' (here 'befall'); پادشا را is the definite object ('the king', marked by را after the noun); بدی is the noun 'misfortune/evil' — the clause reads roughly 'know this: should misfortune befall the king.'
segments.s567-g12: فزاید is a 3rd-person singular present/future/optative of 'to increase' (here 'will/increases'); بدو is the dative clitic phrase 'to him/unto him'; فره ایزدی is a classical compound 'farr-e izadi' = 'divine glory/splendour' (the theme that increases). Poetic word order places the verb before the thing that increases and the beneficiary.
segments.s567-g13: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'; دیدند is the past tense 3rd-person plural 'they saw'; گفتندش is 'they said to him' (گفتند + enclitic -ش = to him); ای پادشا is the vocative 'O king' addressing the monarch directly.
segments.s567-g14: A string of epithets/appellations in nominal form: جهانگیر can be read as an epithet or title 'world-conqueror' (also a proper name), روشن‌دل 'clear-hearted/bright-minded' and پارسا 'pious/virtuous'; و simply coordinates them. These are vocative attributes applied to the king.
segments.s567-g15: یکی is an indefinite 'a certain/one'; کار پیش است is an idiomatic phrase 'a task lies ahead' (پیش است = 'is before/coming'); با رنج و درد is an adjunct of manner 'with hardship and pain', describing the nature of that forthcoming task.
segments.s567-g16: This line is compact and poetically inverted. نیارد here is an archaic/poetic negative verb form (contracted from نیاورد) meaning 'will not bring' / 'will not perform' (used impersonally); کس with that negative reads 'no one'; آن is the demonstrative 'that'; بر + تو (appearing here as بر توبر in the text) marks the beneficiary 'for you / upon you' (poetic attachment/word-order causes the clitic-like shape); یاد کرد ('did remember' / 'take note') functions as an exhortation 'remember/keep in mind.' Overall the sense is 'no one will do that for you — remember this.' Because of dialectal/poetic compression, some word forms are irregular.
segments.s567-g41: چنین گفت = 'he said thus' (چنین = 'thus/so' precedes گفت for emphasis). کاین is an archaic contracted form of که این ('that this'), introducing the reported clause. پادشاهی is the noun 'kingship/kingdom' and به داد is a prepositional phrase meaning 'with justice / in justice' (به + abstract noun داد). Word order is flexible and emphatic: the reporting verb گفت plus the following clause introduced by ک(ه).
segments.s567-g42: بدارید is the 2nd-person plural imperative of داشتن/داستن meaning 'keep/maintain' (here an order addressed to listeners). کزداد is the clause-initial ک(ه) + زاد/زیاد (poetic/contracted) expressing purpose/result 'that it increase' (an optative/subjunctive idea in older Persian). باشید is the 2nd-person plural subjunctive/imperative form of بودن used as a jussive 'may you be', and شاد is the predicate adjective 'happy'.
segments.s567-g43: شتر is the direct object 'camel(s)' placed before the verb in this clause. خواست is the simple past 'he wanted/ordered'. پرمایه is an adjective 'well‑provided / richly laden' describing the camels. The numeral ده ('ten') in the line (printed in the segment text) quantifies the camels; classical Persian often puts numerals and modifiers in variable order.
segments.s567-g44: به هر کاروان = 'to/for each caravan' (به = 'to/for', هر = 'each', کاروان = 'caravan'). بر here marks 'per' or 'over/in charge of' and introduces allocation: بر یکی ساروان = 'one (یکی) sārvān (caravan‑leader) per caravan.' The numeral-like one (یکی) precedes the noun, a common older word order.
segments.s567-g45: ز here is the poetic/prepositional 'from/of' introducing the materials. The sequence ز دینار وز گوهران should be read as 'with/from dinars and with/from gems' (وز here is a fused form of و + ز = 'and from'), and بار کرد is a light-verb construction 'made/loaded (a) load' — i.e. 'he loaded (them) with ...'. The verb کرد is the past tense 'did/made' taking the preceding noun بار as its object.
segments.s567-g46: ازان سی شتر = 'of those, thirty camels' (ازان = 'from those/of those', سی = 'thirty', شتر = 'camels'); word order places the quantifier phrase before the verb. بار دینار کرد repeats the light‑verb construction: بار (load) + دینار (what the load consisted of) + کرد (he made/did) = 'he loaded with dinars' or 'he made carry loads of dinars.'
segments.s567-g47: بیامد is the simple past 'he came'. پراندیشه is a compound/adjective 'full of thought' = 'thoughtful' and functions as a predicate or descriptive phrase attached to the subject implied by the verb. ز آبادبوم = 'from the prosperous/native land' (ز = 'from', آبادبوم = 'prosperous dwelling / homeland'). The clause is compact and typical of classical Persian: verb + descriptive phrase + origin.
segments.s567-g48: همی رفت is an older progressive/formal phrasing meaning 'he went / was going' (همی adds continuity/emphasis). زین سان is a fixed phrase 'in this way / thus' (زین + سان = 'this manner'), سوی = 'toward', مرز روم = 'the border/frontier of Rome' (روم = Byzantium/the Romans). Word order is adverbial phrase (زین سان) + direction (سوی ...) following the verb.
segments.s567-g57: سپیده برآمد is a simple past narrative: سپیده (dawn) is the subject and برآمد is the past-tense verb 'arose/appeared'. بنه برنهاد is another past action: بنه here means 'a small bundle/pack', and برنهاد is the past of نهادن/نَهَادَن (to place/raise/put on) — together 'he lifted/took up the bundle'. The two clauses are juxtaposed as consecutive actions at dawn.
segments.s567-g58: سوی خانه قیصر آمد is a prepositional phrase + past verb: سوی + noun phrase (خانه قیصر) = 'toward the Caesar's house', and آمد is past 'came/went'. چو باد is a simile: چو = 'like/as' and باد = 'wind', giving 'like the wind' (i.e. swiftly).
segments.s567-g59: بیامد is past 'he came' (بیامد = آمد). به نزدیک frames the motion as 'to/near' (near/close to), and سالار بار is a compound title: سالار = chief/commander and بار = load/caravan, so 'the caravan-chief' or 'master of the load'. The whole phrase means 'he came close to the caravan-master.'
segments.s567-g60: آفرین کرد is a light-verb construction: آفرین (an exclamation of praise, 'bravo' or 'well done') + کرد (did/made) = 'he uttered praise' or 'he praised/exclaimed “afrin”'. برو here functions as an interjectional particle preceding the praise (colloquially intensifying the utterance), and و links the next clause. بردش is the past of بردن with enclitic -ش ('him/it') = 'he took/carried/accepted it (or him)'; نثار is a noun 'offering/dedication' and is the object/role of what was taken. Together: 'he praised him and took it as an offering.'
segments.s567-g61: بپرسید is past 'he asked' (3rd person). و simply connects the two verbs. گفتش is 'he said to him' (گفت + enclitic -ش). چه مردی is an interrogative phrase 'what sort of man' (چه = what/what kind of; مردی = 'man' or 'a man of such sort'), and بگوی here is a jussive/imperative-type form meaning 'say/tell' (i.e. 'tell me'). The clause is thus 'he asked and said to him: “Tell me what kind of man he is.”'
segments.s567-g62: که introduces the content/relative clause 'who/that'. هم ... و هم is the correlative pair 'both ... and ...'. شاه‌شاخی is a compound meaning 'of royal stock/lineage' (شاه = king, شاخی used here as an attributive form), and شاه‌روی similarly means 'kingly-faced' or 'of royal countenance/appearance'. The clause qualifies the kind of man being asked about.
segments.s567-g63: چنین داد پاسخ is a fixed narrative formula: چنین = 'thus/so', داد = 'gave' (past), and پاسخ = 'answer' — 'thus he gave answer' = 'he replied'. که introduces the quoted speech that follows; ای پادشا is a vocative 'O King' (ای = O, پادشا = king), addressed to the ruler.
segments.s567-g64: یکی is an indefinite 'one/a certain' used to introduce the man. پارسی here identifies his ethnicity/status as 'Persian' (of Persia). مردم in this phrase functions as 'of the people' or 'a man of the people' (describing social character), and پارسا is an adjective/noun meaning 'pious/devout'. Together the phrase is a concise description: 'a certain Persian, a man of the people and pious.'
segments.s567-g105: چو here is a subordinating conjunction introducing a conditional/time clause ('when/if'). زین باره is a fixed, somewhat literary phrase meaning 'on this occasion / in this matter' (زین is an archaic demonstrative + باره 'occasion'). دانش نیاید is a negative present/subjunctive/predictive clause: نیاید is the classical form of 'does not come/arrive' and takes دانش as its subject; به بر is a locative/benefactive phrase ('to/for the matter' or 'upon the case') that links this subordinate clause to the main question that follows.
segments.s567-g106: This is the main interrogative clause. چه is the interrogative 'what'; باید is a modal 'must/ought' used rhetorically; شمار is a deverbal/nominal noun 'counting' (the act of counting) and functions as the thing whose usefulness is questioned; ستاره‌شمر is a compound noun 'star‑counter' (astrologer) modifying or possessor of that counting. Word order is typical Persian: interrogative + modal + noun phrase (the agent/possessor).
segments.s567-g107: بر introduces the state or setting ('in/on'), followed by مست 'intoxicated' describing the state. شمعی is an adjectival/simile form ('candle‑like' or 'like a candle') formed with the -ی suffix. همی + سوختند is a literary continuous past: همی marks ongoing or habitual action and سوختند is the past plural of 'to burn' (here in a figurative/experiential sense 'they burned' or 'they pined/consumed themselves').
segments.s567-g108: به زاریش is a prepositional phrase 'for his/ because of his lament' (‑ش is 3rd‑person possessive). در here is the noun 'door' (not the preposition), چرم خر is a genitive compound 'donkey‑leather' (the hide of a donkey), and دوختند is the past plural 'they sewed/stitched'. The clause therefore means they sewed donkey-hide onto the door because of his lament — object (چرم خر) appears before the verb, as usual in Persian.
segments.s567-g109: همی گفت is a formulaic literary expression meaning 'people used to say' or 'it was said' (همی marks habitual/ongoing past, گفت is the past of 'to say'). هرکس که introduces a free relative/indefinite clause 'whoever/anyone who', and این شوربخت is the noun phrase 'this unfortunate one / this wretch' which is the referent of that clause. (Note: here گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' rather than the noun sense given in the wordlist.)
segments.s567-g110: همی again marks continued/habitual past. پوست خر is a noun phrase 'the donkey's skin/hide' (خر as genitive). جست is a literary past form of جستن used here in a transitive/causative sense ('took off / pulled out' or 'took'); this is an archaic/poetic usage not fully matched by the simple senses listed. و connects the next clause; بگذاشت is past 'he/she put/placed' (بَ‑ prefix + گذاشت) and تخت here is 'bed' (object 'he placed on the bed'). The two verbs form a coordinated sequence: (he) took the donkey‑hide and placed it on the bed.
segments.s567-g111: یکی خانه‌ای is an indefinite noun phrase 'a certain house' or 'one house' (یکی used to introduce an indefinite entity; خانه‑ای has the indefinite suffix -ی). بود is the past copula 'was'. The adjectives تاریک و تنگ follow the noun and jointly describe it ('dark and narrow'). This is straightforward descriptive word order: NP + copula + adjective phrase.
segments.s567-g112: ببردند is the past plural 'they took' (بر + بردند). بدبخت را is the definite/direct object 'the unfortunate (man)' marked with را. بی‌درنگ is an adverb 'immediately' modifying the verb. The clause is SOV: they (understood subject) took the unfortunate man immediately (to that house / inside).
segments.s567-g49: یکی here means 'a certain' (not the numeral). The clause is a simple past Existential: روستا بود = 'there was a village'; نزدیک شهر is an adverbial phrase 'near the city' placed after the copula for poetic word order.
segments.s567-g50: که introduces a relative clause modifying 'village' ('which'). دهقان و شهری lists two kinds of inhabitants ('peasant and townsman'); بدو is an old postpositional sequence meaning 'to it' / 'belonging to it', followed by بود (past copula). بهر is a poetic preposition meaning 'for' or 'as regards' the village.
segments.s567-g51: بیامد is the past tense 'came' placed at the start (typical in epic style). به خان is a prepositional phrase 'to the khan/inn'; یکی کدخدای is the post-verbal subject 'a certain village-headman.'
segments.s567-g52: بپرسید is the reporting verb 'he asked' (past). What follows is an embedded question: کاید مرا هست جای = 'Is there a place for me?'. مرا is here split orthographically into م + را (m + object marker), and هست is the existential/copular verb 'is/exists'. The token کاید in this line is an archaic/interrogative form introducing the question; it is not the noun 'plot/cunning' listed in the glosses.
segments.s567-g53: This clause reports the chief's reaction. آفرین کرد is 'gave praise' (کرد = did/made + آفرین = praise/exclamation). مهتر is the agent 'the chief/elder'. The initial برو here is an archaic or contracted particle (not the modern imperative 'go'); it functions as a vocative/particle linked to the act of praising and is best read as an older idiom rather than literal 'go'.
segments.s567-g54: که introduces reported content: چون تو نیابیم مهمان کسی = 'for/that we do not (shall not) find a guest like you.' چون = 'like/as'; نیابیم is a classical-form verb (negative + subjunctive/optative shape used in subordinate clause) meaning 'we (shall) not find' or 'we cannot find'; کسی here means 'anyone' (i.e. 'no one else').
segments.s567-g55: ببود is an archaic past copula 'there was' / 'he was' used for narrative past. آن شب 'that night' is the time expression; و connects verbs: خورد 'he ate' and بخشید 'he gave/bestowed' something (چیز). The string lists actions performed that night in narrative past.
segments.s567-g56: ز = 'from' (poetic form of از). دهقان is 'the peasant' (agent/source of praise); بسی + آفرین = 'much praise' (بسی as an intensive 'very/much'); یافت is past 'obtained/received'; نیز is 'also/likewise' tying this result to the previous actions.
segments.s567-g1: چنان بد که is a fixed comparative/result clause: چنان (so) + بد (bad/wretched) + که (that) introduces the following circumstance. یک روز (literally “one day”) functions adverbially here — “on a day/once” — and با تاج و گنج is a prepositional phrase (با = with) listing possessions (تاج = crown, گنج = treasure) that qualify the surprising condition introduced by چنان بد که.
segments.s567-g2: همی داشت combines همی (an older continuous/emphatic adverb 'still/constantly') with داشت (past imperfect/continuous ‘was having’ or ‘kept’), giving a progressive meaning: ‘kept / was continually having’ or ‘was continually in the state of’. از بودنی gives the reason/cause clause ('from/of being' i.e. ‘because of existence’), دل is the affected noun 'heart' and به رنج is a predicative phrase 'in suffering' — together: ‘his heart was continually in suffering because of being.’
segments.s567-g3: ز here is the poetic form of از ('from/after'). تیره شب is a noun phrase 'the dark night' and اندر گذشته (literally 'within/passed') is a participial phrase meaning 'having passed' or 'after ... had gone by.' سه پاس ('three watches') names the duration (پاس = a night watch). The whole segment means 'after three watches of the dark night had passed.'
segments.s567-g4: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past tense verb meaning 'he commanded/ordered.' تا here functions in the temporal/result sense ('until' / 'so that') linking the ordering to its outcome; شد is the past of شدن ('became'), and ستاره‌شناس is the result noun 'stargazer/astrologer' (in this poetic context meaning one who studies or interprets the stars). Grammatically this is a causative/result clause: he ordered (بفرمود) ... until/so that (تا) he became (شد) an astrologer (ستاره‌شناس).
segments.s567-g5: بپرسیدش is an imperative/polite present-form verb 'ask him' (بپرسید + object clitic -ش). از governs the topic 'about/concerning'; تخت شاهنشهی is a noun phrase 'the imperial throne' (تخت = throne, شاهنشهی describing its imperial character). The structure is 'Ask him about the imperial throne.'
segments.s567-g6: هم = 'also' links this second request to the first. از again marks 'about/regarding.' رنج means 'suffering/hardship' as a topic. وز is an archaic fused particle (و + از) used poetically to link items and here functions like 'and/of' connecting رنج and روزگار; it is an old-fashioned form and does not match the modern sense list directly. روزگار = 'the times' or 'the course of days' and بهی here means 'good/excellent' or 'well-being,' so the phrase asks about 'suffering and the state/fortune of the times.'
segments.s567-g7: منجم (subject) = 'the astrologer' and بیاورد is a narrative/past tense verb 'brought' (classical form). صلاب (object) means 'firmness/rigidity' and is marked by the object marker را, so the clause literally reads 'the astrologer brought the firmness.' Word order here is subject–verb–object with را marking the definite object.
segments.s567-g8: بینداخت is the simple past transitive verb 'he threw/cast' (or 'overturned'). آرامش و خواب را is the coordinated object ('calm/tranquility and sleep') with را at the end marking the definite direct object(s). Together: 'he cast away/overthrew calm and sleep.'

sentences.s567-s69: No woman, man, or child remained in Iran; nothing — neither great nor small — was left.
sentences.s567-s70: There was no news in the army; neither dead nor alive (was known) concerning King Shapur.
sentences.s567-s71: All the cities of Iran fled from the Romans; all borderlands and homesteads became empty of people.
sentences.s567-s72: From Iran they became excessively pious; all the borderlands went before (appeared before) Sekuba.
sentences.s567-s13: May all that exists be without doubt; we cannot endure the revolutions of the sky.
sentences.s567-s14: Thus the noble king gave his answer: 'May the Creator keep us from every evil glance.'
sentences.s567-s15: Who created the lofty, revolving heaven, (also) created the able and the unable.
sentences.s567-s16: He spread justice over his kingdom; for a short time he was untroubled and joyful.
sentences.s567-s45: He said to the Caesar: "O exalted one, hear a new speech from me in secret."
sentences.s567-s46: that this famed man is a merchant who sells brocade to the wealthy.
sentences.s567-s47: I call him Shahanshah Shapur, for he is so in speech, in appearance, in royal glory, and in presence at court.
sentences.s567-s48: When the Caesar heard the words his face darkened, and his eyes became fixed on him.
sentences.s567-s41: When Shapur came near the Caesar, he uttered “Afārīn” in a manner befitting.
sentences.s567-s42: The Caesar cast his gaze toward Shapur; because of his beauty he entrusted (gave) his heart and eyes to him.
sentences.s567-s43: He ordered that they set the table and prepare wine, and they attended to the stranger's iwan.
sentences.s567-s44: The wronged Iranian was ill-treated by Rome; just as (it was), the man was cruel and ill‑omened.
sentences.s567-s37: I will sell the rest for gold and silver; I will not, out of fear, turn to the Caesar for refuge.
sentences.s567-s38: I'll buy whatever I must from Rum; from Rum I will set out toward Iran, from my prosperous homeland.
sentences.s567-s39: An old man rose from the doorway; he came up to the Caesar and spoke these words.
sentences.s567-s40: He ordered it; they lifted the curtain, and at the doorway they placed him before the Caesar.
sentences.s567-s33: I went to the merchants' market; apart from that I have one caravan of furs and goats.
sentences.s567-s34: Now I have come to this court, hoping to find someone at the emperor's side to open a way.
sentences.s567-s35: From this load there is nothing fit to eat inside; all is jewels and the gear of an army.
sentences.s567-s36: Let him accept it and entrust the treasure to the treasury; I shall be glad of that — I have no need of toil.
sentences.s567-s17: When, through him, all the borderlands and the country became prosperous, he made such a wish that he might go to Rome.
sentences.s567-s18: He wished to see whether Caesar was worthy — whether he should have an army, treasure, and military strength.
sentences.s567-s19: He disclosed that same secret to the village headman, and told him to muster a single champion, with justice and counsel.
sentences.s567-s20: He told him all the secrets and thoughts, and kept them hidden from everyone else.
sentences.s567-s65: He handed over the key of his house; Shapur wrapped (him) in leather and shut (him) up inside.
sentences.s567-s66: That same day he drove the army from that frontier, and (having) bound him in a hide, left him there.
sentences.s567-s67: When Caesar came near Iran, the host drew their swords one by one for vengeance.
sentences.s567-s68: From Iran the Romans carried off captives; there was no one to seize those heroes.
sentences.s567-s61: That very throne of Caesar did not come to his mind; there was nowhere anyone of Caesar's lineage.
sentences.s567-s62: Caesar's wife shut the door of that house and sat down in another place on the iwan.
sentences.s567-s63: There was a certain moon-faced beauty, rich in treasures; a select one, and in every affair her command.
sentences.s567-s64: for she was of the Iranians' stock — she could trace her ancestry father upon father in memory.
sentences.s567-s57: They threw him into that narrow place and fastened a lock on the house door.
sentences.s567-s58: They gave his key to the housekeeper and delivered his body to that stranger's hide.
sentences.s567-s59: He told the woman, 'Give him so much bread and water that she will not be quick to deprive him.'
sentences.s567-s60: If he lives for a short while, he will come to know the value (honor) of the throne and crown.
sentences.s567-s9: Thus Shapur gave the original reply: "O knowledgeable man and seeker of the road."
sentences.s567-s10: What remedy is there until this passes from me? An ill star did not entrust my body to follow.
sentences.s567-s11: The astrologer said, "O king, from this unstable turning of the heavens..."
sentences.s567-s12: You will not gain passage by manliness and learning, if the wise man is quarrelsome.
sentences.s567-s49: His guard rose and spoke to no one; he kept that secret hidden.
sentences.s567-s50: When Shapur the king became drunk he rose; Caesar kept his gaze upon him.
sentences.s567-s51: The guard came and seized him, (saying) 'O Shapur, do not go among such a wonder.'
sentences.s567-s52: He took him to the women's quarters and bound his hands; no man sought to free him from the snare of calamity.
sentences.s567-s5: He cast a bright look into Asad's heart, for he is the representative of conquest and earnestness.
sentences.s567-s6: Know this: should misfortune befall the king, divine glory will increase to him.
sentences.s567-s7: When they saw (him), they said to him, 'O king, world-conqueror, clear‑hearted and pious.'
sentences.s567-s8: There is a task ahead that will involve toil and pain; no one will do that for you — keep that in mind.
sentences.s567-s21: He said thus: "Keep this kingship in justice; may it increase and may you be happy."
sentences.s567-s22: He desired camels — ten well‑laden for each caravan; to every caravan one caravan‑leader (sārvān).
sentences.s567-s23: He loaded (them) with dinars and with gems; of those, thirty camels he made carry dinars.
sentences.s567-s24: The thoughtful man came from his prosperous homeland and thus went toward the frontier of Rome.
sentences.s567-s29: Dawn rose; he took up his bundle and, like the wind, went toward the Caesar's house.
sentences.s567-s30: He came up close to the caravan-chief; he praised him and took (or accepted) a gift as an offering.
sentences.s567-s31: He asked and said to him, 'Describe what sort of man he is,' — that he is both of royal lineage and of royal visage.
sentences.s567-s32: Thus he gave answer: 'O King, a certain Persian, a man of the people and devout.'
sentences.s567-s53: If knowledge does not come to this matter, what good is it for the astrologer to count?
sentences.s567-s54: In their intoxication they burned like candles; for his lament they sewed donkey-hide to the door.
sentences.s567-s55: People used to say that whoever encountered this wretch would take the donkey's hide and lay it on the bed.
sentences.s567-s56: There was a certain house, dark and narrow; they immediately took the unfortunate man there.
sentences.s567-s25: There was a certain village near the city, which had both peasants and townsmen attached to it.
sentences.s567-s26: A certain village-head came to the khan and asked, "Is there a place for me?"
sentences.s567-s27: The chief exclaimed praise abundantly, saying that he would not find any guest like you.
sentences.s567-s28: That night he stayed, ate, and gave out something; from the peasant he also received much praise.
sentences.s567-s1: He was so wretched that, even on a day when he possessed crown and treasure, his heart was ever in anguish because of existence.
sentences.s567-s2: When three watches of the dark night had passed, he gave orders, and thus became an astrologer.
sentences.s567-s3: Ask him about the imperial throne, and also about suffering and the fortunes of the times.
sentences.s567-s4: The astrologer brought firmness; he cast away calm and sleep.
